notes.claim: Labor mobilization was carried out based on the National General Mobilization Act (1938).
notes.context: Promulgated on April 1, 1938. A law that allows the state to control and mobilize human and material resources in times of war. It was gradually applied to the Korean Peninsula starting in 1939.

sources.title: 国家総動員法
sources.publisher: 国立公文書館デジタルアーカイブ

notes.claim: Personnel were secured through the stages of "recruitment" in 1939, "official mediation" in 1942, and "requisition" in 1944.
notes.context: The mobilization of Korean workers was carried out in three stages. From 1939, recruitment by companies was applied, from 1942 official mediation through administrative agencies, and from September 1944 legal recruitment based on the National Requisition Ordinance was applied.

sources.title: 朝鮮人強制連行の記録

notes.claim: “Completely and finally resolved” by the Japan-Korea Claims Agreement of 1965
notes.context: Signed June 22, 1965. Japan provides South Korea with $300 million in grants and $200 million in fees. Article 2 of the agreement stipulates that the property and claims between the two countries have been ``completely and finally resolved.'' However, interpretations differ between Japan and South Korea regarding the scope of this agreement.

sources.title: 日韓請求権並びに経済協力協定
Agreement on the Settlement of Problems Concerning Property and Claims

notes.claim: South Korean Supreme Court judgment in 2018 ordered Japanese companies to pay compensation
notes.context: On October 30, 2018, the South Korean Supreme Court ordered Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (now Nippon Steel) to pay compensation of 100 million won each to four former conscripted workers. It has been determined that the right to claim compensation for damages based on the illegality of colonial rule is not covered by the Claims Agreement.

sources.title: South Korean court orders Nippon Steel to compensate forced wartime laborers
旧朝鮮半島出身労働者問題に関する外務大臣談話

notes.context: Numbers vary depending on the researcher and source. According to Japanese government data, approximately 720,000 people are subject to labor mobilization to mainland Japan. South Korean estimates may suggest even higher numbers for the wider range of ``forced mobilization.''
notes.verdict: There is a wide range of estimates.

sources.title: 日本における朝鮮人の強制連行・強制労働
sources.publisher: 日本学術振興会（J-STAGE掲載論文）

notes.claim: They were forced to work in harsh conditions on Hashima Island (Gunkanjima) and other places.
notes.context: Hashima is a World Cultural Heritage Site in Nagasaki Prefecture (registered in 2015 as a component of the "Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution"). During the registration review, South Korea objected that the history of Korean forced labor was not sufficiently communicated. Japan has promised to establish an information center.

sources.title: Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution

notes.claim: “Hanaoka Incident” in which Chinese workers revolted at Hanaoka Mine
notes.context: On June 30, 1945, Chinese workers revolted at the Hanaoka Mine in Hanaoka Town (now Odate City), Akita Prefecture. It is believed that more than 400 of the 800 people died. In 2000, a settlement was reached between Kajima Corporation and the Chinese victims.

sources.title: 花岡事件を語り継ぐ（大館市）
Chinese, Kajima settle suit on wartime forced labor
